Low Frequency Noise Monitoring System for Temperature Inversions

For 3rd party compliance
Use low cost commercial off the shelf devices along with calibrated engineering grade sound level microphones and linear microphones that measure low frequency noise.

Audio and Video files .WAV, .MP3, .MP4 are supported for compliance reporting.
Request a license key to generate your own PDF reports from your data. (license key is encrypted and locked to your device): https://redpilldispensary.com/ContactForm.html

Distance from Low Frequency Noise Source for Microphone Placement

For a point source (genset approximation) under moderate-strong inversion/downward refraction:
  • ☐ At 100 m: ~6–12 dB less attenuation than neutral (depending on inversion strength).
  • ☐ At 500 m: 10–20+ dB advantage possible for <100 Hz.
  • ☐ At 1 km: Low-freq components can retain 10–30 dB more energy; overall A-weighted may still drop but with prominent low-freq "rumble."
  • ☐ Thermal power plant/low-freq noise: Measurable correlations with distance up to 660 m, influenced by meteorology.
  • ☐ Data centers/generators: Low-freq hum audible >0.5–1 mile under inversion.

If using a device you already own this can be built for under $100 when used unattended for a few hours up to a day. If you need a longer term monitor you’ll need to upgrade the power and weather protection and expect to spend approximately $200.

As an example you can use an old DirectTV/Dish Satellite dish mount or fence as a sturdy monitoring post.

Use the “Contact Form” if you have any questions.  
https://redpilldispensary.com/ContactForm.html

Read more about Weather Inversions here: https://redpilldispensary.com/Inversion%20Check%20Notes.html

The link to the Inversion Checker Tool is here: https://redpilldispensary.com/Inversion%20Check.html
During an inversion, you can test this near your home Air Conditioning Outdoor Condensing Unit.
Take Ambient monitoring recordings when there isn’t a weather inversion to compare the readings against other noise like mowing your lawn.

Most weather inversions take place during the evening, morning or nighttime, you can use your existing phone for monitoring, use a thermostat or phone app to ensure that it’s not overheating in the sun.

Start with an outdoor protective case for your phone. The Nanuk 330 is typically under $40 and fits most cellphones. The built in holder keeps your phone mounted securely but you’ll want to modify the case by drilling a hole and adding a grommet or silicon for the external power cable and microphone to securely stick out of the bottom of the case. *Make sure nothing is loose and rattles which will ruin your readings.



This should be secured to your own property or installed with written permission from property owners. It should be left to monitor during weather inversions when the sun isn’t shining on it which is going to overheat it if unprotected. If you need a daytime monitor, consider purchasing a more rugged device or mount this on the underside of a solar panel.

Noise Monitoring App examples with C weighting

Example Apps (test) Price Key Strengths for LFN/Infrasound Limitations Best For
FreqCheck : See Sound Paid Specifically built for 1–100 Hzinfrasound/hum measurement. Real-time spectrum view focused on low frequencies. Claims NIST-traceable / ISO-aligned accuracy. Newer app, fewer long-term reviews Best direct alternative for your use case (AC unit LFN)
PocketNoise: Sound Level Meter Paid (~$5–6 one-time) Real-time dB meter with A/C/Z weighting, FFT spectrum analyzer, continuous logging Not specialized for deep infrasound General LFN monitoring + logging
Decibel X (or similar spectrum apps) Paid upgrade Good spectrum/FFT display, can show down to low frequencies Accuracy varies; not as focused on <20 Hz Quick spectrum checks
NIOSH Sound Level Meter (free from CDC) Free Reliable, calibrated-style measurements with spectrum Limited low-frequency emphasis Basic reliable measurements


For 3rd Party Compliance you’ll want a Calibrated Microphone

If using an iphone with 3.5mm adapter, make sure that its using the external microphone.
Use an app that supports external microphones, use the calibration file from the manufacturer.
When you connect a compatible external mic, the app lets you switch from the built-in microphone to the external one in the Settings menu (this was removed on the latest version). With a good external mic + calibration, the app can meet Type 2 (Class 2) accuracy according to IEC 61672 standards.

Recommended External Microphones for iPhone + NIOSH App

Here are the most compatible and commonly used options:
[Table could not be rendered]


Microphone Low-Freq Extension (20–200 Hz) Flatness / Accuracy IEC 61672 Compliance C-Weighting Suitability Best For
Dayton iMM-6 Excellent (down to ~18 Hz) ±0.5 dB calibrated Not certified (performs well) Good (via software) Budget / portable measurements
MicW i436 Good (starts at 20 Hz) Good (calibrated) Class 2 Good More formal / reliable field use

Be sure to use a compatible Windscreen foam cover for the Microphone and plug any connections that are exposed to the weather. You’ll want to zip tie the foam for fair weather recordings, if its expected to rain, be sure to use a ziplock bag around it and ziptie that, it could have a minimal affect on low frequency recordings, but be sure to test during ambient monitoring.

Compatibility Notes


These microphones are omnidirectional, not directional so they should be pointed toward the ground to keep water from damaging the element.
Recommendations:
  • Use with apps that support calibration files (e.g., NIOSH, AudioTools, REW via interface).
  • For true Class 1 accuracy or legal work, use a dedicated Class 1 microphone + sound level meter.
  • Always verify with a calibrator (1 kHz tone) and load the individual calibration file.
  • In 20–200 Hz, pay attention to the host device’s mic input quality (preamp noise can affect results at very low frequencies).



Recommended Solar + Battery Sizes


This is example of a 5 to 10 Watt USB-C Solar Panel that pairs with many outdoor security cameras and is compatible with charging a battery bank.

Most Foldable camping style solar panels may have an integrated charge controller with USB for directly charging your phone or battery.

[Table could not be rendered]


verify the Amp Hours and Temperature range of the battery pack, make sure it has “passthrough charging” so that it can charge from a solar panel at the same time that it charges your phone. Make sure that it doesn’t exceed its rated power input. For example, don’t plug a 100 watt solar panel into this without using a charge controller but a 10 watt panel is fine but may not run for more than a day or two depending on the amount of sun and clouds and outdoor temperatures. Most inversions occur during the spring and fall months.


Accessories and Adapters

Iphone 11 can be wirelessly charged with Qi, Iphone 12 or later uses Magsafe so that you don’t need an a lightning or USB-C splitter in case your phone doesn’t have a separate microphone port. (check compatibility for Android devices)

Monitoring with GoPro

When using GoPro, make sure the GPS is locked on before recording to add accurate date, time, location and weather information including temperature inversion to your reports.

You can post process the audio or video data from a Gropro by plugging in the microphone and external power to this adapter, be sure to use the official version to maintain the highest quality.

For long-term outdoor recording (especially hours or more), you should remove the internal battery and power the Hero9 directly from an external USB power source. This is the official and community-recommended approach to prevent overheating when recording for hours since it Avoids power switching issues:

With the battery installed, the camera often runs on the internal battery first (even when plugged in) until it drains, then powers off briefly before switching to external power/charging. This interrupts long recordings. If using an external battery, it will shut off immediately when power is lost which could corrupt your data so be sure to handle carefully and monitor your battery voltage. Plus your USB-C solar panel into the external battery.

Alternative recording option

your Hero9 (and enhanced with Labs) supports RAW Audio in Protune settings. This creates a separate .WAV filealongside every video/timelapse MP4 file.
  • Go to Protune → RAW Audio and set it to Low (minimal processing — ideal for your post-processing and C-weighted analysis later).
  • The .WAV files are saved in the same folder as the video, with the same filename.
  • This works in TimeLapse Video mode — you get full-resolution frames (or your chosen interval) + a high-quality, lightly processed audio track that you can extract and use separately.
For your long-term outdoor noise monitoring use case:
  1. Set up a long TimeLapse Video (Labs helps with “Extra Long Time-Lapse” scheduling and triggers).
  2. Enable RAW Audio (Low).
  3. In post-processing (Audacity, Python, etc.), ignore or delete the video track and work only with the .WAV for LCeq / C-weighted averages.
  4. This effectively gives you “audio with occasional video snapshots” — very close to audio-only for compliance work.

On HERO10–13 (this may not work on all models), here’s the process:
  1. Install Labs firmware (manual update via SD card from gopro.github.io/labs).
  2. Set your capture mode first (e.g., TimeLapse Video or Video) — the trigger only starts/stops capture; it doesn’t change modes.
  3. Scan a custom SPL QR code (generated on the Labs site):
    • Go to → https://gopro.github.io/labs/control/spltrigger
    • Adjust:
      • Start Sensitivity (dB threshold to begin recording, e.g., 70–90 dB for noise events).
      • End Sensitivity (dB threshold to stop).
      • Start Delay (seconds before checking audio).
      • Hold Time (seconds to keep recording after sound drops below threshold).
    • Scan the generated QR code with the camera.
  4. On-screen feedback shows when the trigger is active and current SPL readings.
Note: SPL values are uncalibrated (internal mic or your Dayton iMM-6 via Media Mod). Test and tweak thresholds in your environment. It works best with external mics for low-frequency noise monitoring.

Add GoPro Labs motion recording for enhanced security.

Newer advanced scripting (on H11/H12/H13) allows combining multiple conditions (e.g., start on motion AND high sound level), but this is not available on the Hero9/10.

The camera stays powered on (but not recording) and analyzes frames for movement.
When motion exceeds your chosen sensitivity, it starts recording.
It continues for a “Hold Time” after motion stops.
Important limitations:
  • The camera must be stationary (mounted solidly). Camera movement will falsely trigger it.
  • Works in Video, TimeWarp, and TimeLapse Video modes (set the mode manually first).
  • Not reliable in very high frame rates (e.g., avoid 4K60, 2.7K120, 1080p240 for full start/stop functionality).


  1. Install GoPro Labs firmware (if not already done) from gopro.github.io/labs.
  2. Go to the Motion Detection page: → https://gopro.github.io/labs/control/motion/
  3. Configure the QR code:
    • Sensitivity: 1 (low) to 9 (very high) — start around 4–6 for outdoor use and test.
    • Start Delay: Seconds to ignore you walking away (e.g., 10–30s).
    • Hold Time: How long to keep recording after motion stops (e.g., 10–60s).
    • Motion Mask (optional): Seconds to learn static background movement (wind, leaves, etc.).
    • Optional advanced mask: Create a custom mask.pgm file on the SD card to ignore specific areas (e.g., sky). Hero9 supports this.
  4. Scan the generated QR code with your Hero9 (screen must be on and facing the QR).
Test thoroughly outdoors: Wind, shadows, insects, or passing clouds can cause false triggers. Use the mask feature to reduce them.

Automatically upload your data
GoPro Quik app + Cloud Auto Upload (easiest option):
  • You can enable Auto Upload (requires a GoPro subscription).
  • When the camera connects to a known WiFi network and is charging (or on external power), it automatically uploads new files to the GoPro Cloud.
  • You can then download them from the cloud to your phone/PC.
  • Works with your motion-triggered clips + RAW .WAV files.
  • Limitation: Requires reliable WiFi + power. Not ideal for remote outdoor spots without a hotspot or stable network. Original files stay on the SD card.

Post Processing the Audio Files from GoPro Cameras

Software Options. Audacity (free), Reaper DAW ($60), Matlab Python Scripts. Be sure to test what works best for your use case.

Extract audio from MP4 (use Audacity, FFmpeg, or GoPro Quik).

Calibrate: Record a known SPL tone (e.g., calibrator at 94 dB) and adjust offset in software for accurate dB values.

Focus on low frequencies: C-weighting preserves more bass than A-weighting — perfect for your compliance needs.

For long recordings: Look for "running Leq" or export time-history data to CSV/Excel for averages over periods.

Security Cameras with Microphone Input

These haven’t been tested but if the files can be read and post processed like the GoPro files they should work as well.
Brands and Models with 3.5mm Mic Ports
  • Axis Communications: Many models in the Axis M, P, and Q-Line (e.g., AXIS P1343, AXIS Q1604, AXIS P3344) support 3.5mm microphone inputs.   The AXIS T8351 and AXIS T8353A are dedicated external microphones designed for these cameras. 
  • Panasonic i-Pro and Bosch: These brands offer professional-grade cameras with line-in capabilities, typically using 3.5mm jacks, RCA connectors, or terminal blocks for external audio. 
  • Security Camera King and CCTV Camera Pros: These retailers offer various IP cameras with 3.5mm mic inputs, often sold as part of a surveillance system that includes compatible microphones.

Compatible Sound Recorders (post process the .wav or .mp3 files)


Here are the most affordable options that reliably work with TRRS mics like the i436 (prices approximate/current market; check retailers like Amazon, B&H, or local stores for deals):
  • Cheapest viable options (~$20–60):
    • Basic digital voice recorders with 3.5mm mic input (e.g., generic or Sony ICD-PX series entry models, or similar USB-rechargeable dictaphones). Many support external mics and are sufficient for simple recording/measurement if you use apps or built-in features. Look for ones explicitly mentioning external mic support and plug-in power.
    • Sony ICD-UX570 or similar budget models (~$50–100): Compact, good built-in features, 3.5mm stereo mic-in jack. Solid entry for measurement with the i436.
  • Best low-priced dedicated field recorder (~$100–150):
    • Zoom H1 Essential or H1n (~$100–120): Highly recommended entry-level. 3.5mm stereo mic/line input with plug-in power, 32-bit float on newer models (great for measurement/dynamic range), compact, and widely compatible. Excellent choice for pairing with the i436 for accurate recordings.
  • Slightly higher but still affordable (~$150+):
    • Zoom H1 XLR or Tascam DR-05XP/DR-40X entry kits: Offer more inputs and better preamps if you expand later.


Securing the equipment

  • ☐ Utilize a locking screw like a torx with large threads on the case latch. Make sure to predrill and test.
  • ☐ Microphone height: Typically 1.2–1.5 m (receiver height) which is within reach.
  • ☐ Dim the screen, make sure its not set to sleep and set a passcode and enable features like “Find my Iphone” or use a hidden Airtag.
  • ☐ Consider finding an app that runs in the background that records when it detects motion, and could email or text alerts. A monitoring system that tracks date, time, gps from multiple sources could be utilized for providing active mitigation or warning systems.
  • ☐ Verify against a Class 1 sound level meter for legal compliance.
  • ☐ Always Test and Test Again

Compliance Standards and Best Practices

Best Practices for Microphone Recording (Aligned with ISO 9613-2 Context)

  1. Microphone and System Requirements:
    • Use Class 1 (precision) sound level meters or microphones compliant with IEC 61672-1 for accuracy in environmental measurements.
    • Free-field or random-incidence microphones, depending on setup (free-field preferred for outdoor propagation).
  2. Sampling and Bandwidth:
    • Sample at ≥ 2× highest frequency of interest (e.g., ≥ 40 kHz for 20 kHz coverage) to satisfy Nyquist and allow anti-aliasing filters.
    • Record uncompressed time-series data (e.g., WAV at 24-bit/48 kHz or higher) for post-processing into octave bands.
    • Ensure flat frequency response across the target range; calibrate regularly.
  3. Measurement Setup for Propagation Studies:
    • Microphone height: Typically 1.2–1.5 m (receiver height) or as specified for validation against predictions.
    • Downwind or inversion-favorable conditions for "worst-case" propagation matching the standard.
    • Multiple positions: Near-source (for emission) and far-field (for attenuation verification).
    • Wind screens essential outdoors to reduce turbulence noise (especially important at low frequencies).
  4. Additional Considerations:
    • Low-frequency emphasis: For generator or industrial sources (as in prior context), extend to 20–31.5 Hz if infrasound or ducting under inversion is relevant.
    • Duration: Record long enough for equivalent continuous levels (L_eq or L_T) under stable meteorology.
    • Post-processing: Apply C-weighting per the standard for comparison with predictions.
    • Limitations: The method is for point sources/assemblies; not for aircraft, blasts, or highly refractive conditions outside favorable meteorology.
This range and approach ensure recordings are directly usable for input (source characterization) or validation (received levels) in ISO 9613-2:2024 models. For full implementation, consult the complete standard (available via ISO or national bodies) alongside ISO 1996 for environmental noise descriptors.
Report both dB(C) and dB(A) for low-frequency dominant sources.
Use 1/3-octave or narrowband FFT analysis down to 1–20 Hz for detailed diagnosis (especially under inversion conditions where low frequencies propagate far).
Many guidelines (e.g., for wind farms, power plants, or industrial sites) explicitly recommend C-weighting or G-weighting when low-frequency complaints arise.

Monitoring Industrial Generators

Industrial synchronous generators (driven by diesel engines) produce 50 Hz (Europe/Asia, 1500 RPM for 4-pole) or 60 Hz(North America, 1800 RPM for 4-pole) AC power. This is the primary electrical output frequency.
In vibration/acoustic FFT analysis (most relevant for "low frequency" + FFT queries):
  • Rotational frequency (1× RPM): Often the dominant low-frequency peak.
    • 25 Hz (1500 RPM / 50 Hz gensets).
    • 30 Hz (1800 RPM / 60 Hz gensets).
  • Firing frequency (combustion impulses, multi-cylinder diesel): Typically 2× or higher orders of rotational speed (e.g., ~50 Hz or multiples for common 4-stroke engines).
  • 2× line frequency (2× LF): Electromagnetically dominant at 100 Hz (50 Hz systems) or 120 Hz (60 Hz systems). This is a strong peak in vibration spectra due to magnetic forces.
  • Other low-frequency harmonics: Unbalance/misalignment (1× or 2× RPM), low-frequency flow noise (~0.8–33 Hz).

Monitoring Wind Turbine Low Frequency Noise with these devices is NOT Recommended, its not in hearing range.

Key Frequencies (Typical Modern 3-Bladed Onshore Turbines)

  • Blade Passing Frequency (BPF): Usually 0.5–1.2 Hz (infrasound).
    • Formula: BPF = (Rotor rotational speed in RPM / 60) × Number of blades.
    • Example: 15 RPM × 3 blades = 0.75 Hz (one pressure pulse every ~1.33 seconds).
    • Common range for utility-scale turbines (1–5+ MW): 0.6–1 Hz at normal operation.
  • Harmonics of BPF: Strong peaks at 2×, 3×, ... up to ~10–20 Hz (and sometimes higher). These are the most characteristic and dominant low-frequency signatures in FFT spectra
